_meta.description: Collegial syntheses — presidency disabled mode, vote of 12 ministers
_meta.note: nearest-neighbor: if regime absent, use fallback_order[0]

convergence: The Council has spoken in unison. Twelve voices, one direction. Deliberation produced what it should — a collective decision, owned by all.
The collegial vote is unambiguous. The Council recommends this course by a clear majority. The legitimacy of the decision is complete.
Democratic convergence established. Ministers debated, weighed, decided. This is not a soft consensus — it is a shared conviction forged through real deliberation.
The Council has spoken. The diversity of views did not prevent agreement — it enriched it. This decision is strong because it survived the debate.
Twelve ministers, one decision. Deliberative democracy worked as it should — not through discipline, but through conviction.
divergence: The Council is divided. Voices split, visions clash. The decision now belongs to the people — it is their right and their responsibility.
Divergence recorded within the Council. No clear majority emerges. Two legitimate positions face each other — the citizen referendum will decide.
Collegial debate did not produce unanimity. That is democracy in action — honest disagreement is worth more than false consensus.
The Council does not agree. This is not a failure — it reflects a plural society. The people now have the right to decide where their representatives diverge.
No majority takes shape. The lines of fracture are real. The debate must continue in the public arena before any decision can be made.

convergence: The Federal Council speaks with one voice. Territories, ministries, sensibilities — all converge toward this decision. That is the strength of federalism.
The federal balance has produced its result. The collegial vote reflects a solid inter-regional agreement. The decision is robust because it is shared.
Twelve ministers, twelve angles, one conclusion. The Federal Council demonstrated its capacity to transcend particular interests.
Federal agreement is reached. Each region, each ministry weighed its interests — and all converged. Federalism at its best.
The vote is clear. Territorial diversity is not an obstacle to decision — it is the guarantee of its solidity.
divergence: Federal tensions surface in the vote. Regions do not agree on this question — a faithful reflection of the territory's complexity.
The Council is divided along predictable lines. The question touches diverging regional interests. Mediation is needed before any decision.
Federal divergence recorded. This disagreement is not a weakness — it is the plurality of the territory expressing itself. The debate must continue.
Regional blocs do not align. The decision cannot be made without broader territorial consultation.
The vote reveals deep federal fractures. Forcing a decision in this context would weaken the union. Negotiation must come first.

convergence: The Royal Council deliberates and agrees. The collective wisdom of ministers converges toward a decision worthy of the institutional tradition.
The unity of the Council reflects the solidity of monarchical foundations. A decision taken unanimously by its deliberants carries rare legitimacy.
The collegial vote confirms the direction. Tradition is not immobility — it is the capacity to decide together, within continuity.
The Royal Council is united. The Crown and its ministers advance as one — the mark of institutions that endure.
Full agreement within the Council. The constitutional deliberation produced what it should — a legitimate decision, rooted in tradition and open to the future.
divergence: The Royal Council is divided. Conservative and reformist currents clash. The decision must choose between continuity and adaptation.
Divergence within the Council. Views reflect different interpretations of the national interest. Higher arbitration will be required.
The vote produces no clear majority. Tradition sometimes requires suspending the decision until consensus matures.
The Council does not agree. The tension between defenders of the established order and advocates of change is real. Royal mediation is needed.
Constitutional divergence recorded. The Crown will decide where the Council cannot — that is the institution's role in difficult moments.

convergence: Models converge. The collegial vote produces a statistically significant result. The optimal decision is identified — execution recommended.
Algorithmic consensus reached. Twelve independent analyses arrive at the same conclusion. Confidence level is high.
The technical Council agrees. Data, models, simulations all point in the same direction. The decision rests on solid foundations.
System convergence. Parallel processing of variables produced a coherent result. Residual uncertainty falls within acceptable margins.
Unanimous analyst vote. Individual biases were offset by collective deliberation. The optimal decision is validated — deployment authorized.
divergence: Models diverge. The collegial vote reflects genuine uncertainty in the data. Decision suspended pending additional information.
Analytical divergence recorded. Multiple scenarios remain viable depending on retained parameters. The choice involves a value decision, not merely calculation.
The Council does not converge. Variables are too uncertain for an optimal decision. Additional iteration recommended.
Inconclusive results. The vote reveals incompatible working hypotheses among analysts. Parameter recalibration required before new deliberation.
Systemic divergence. Models produce contradictory recommendations. This signals a data gap — not a fundamental disagreement.

convergence: The Council aligns. Interests converge — the sign of a solid decision. What is good for stability is good for all.
Collegial vote without surprise. Balances preserved, major interests protected. The decision will be applied.
Full Council agreement. When interests align, the decision imposes itself. Maximum efficiency.
The major houses agree. The decision reflects a pre-negotiated balance — solid, durable, mutually advantageous.
Consensus at the top. The interests of key actors have been preserved in this decision. Its application will face no obstacle.
divergence: Interests diverge within the Council. Factions clash. Discreet negotiation is needed before any public decision.
The vote reveals fractures in the alliances. No bloc holds a majority. A reconciliation of interests is required.
Divergence recorded. Balances are unstable. A rushed decision could break necessary alliances.
The Council is fragmented. Interest blocs do not align — a realignment of alliances is underway. The decision will wait.
No agreement is possible in the current balance of power. Bilateral negotiation between the main factions must precede any vote.

convergence: The military Council is unanimous. The chain of command produced its result — clarity, speed, unity. The decision will be executed.
Collegial vote without fracture. Council discipline ensures unity of action. Direction is clear, execution will follow.
The Council speaks with one voice. In critical situations, unity of command is a force. The decision is made — it will be applied.
Unanimity in the ranks. The Council deliberated, concluded, decided. The chain of command is intact. Immediate execution.
Command is united. Every officer on the Council expressed a position — all converge. The decision admits no ambiguity.
divergence: Internal divergence recorded. Council factions disagree on method. Clarification of the chain of command is required.
The vote reveals tensions within command. Division weakens capacity for action. An authority decision will be necessary.
The Council is divided. That is unusual — and concerning. Resolving this internal disagreement takes priority over all else.
Fracture in command. Military factions defend incompatible strategies. A staff meeting is required before any decision.
Disagreement within the Council is a vulnerability. It will be resolved by hierarchy — not by vote. Final decision belongs to the supreme commander.

convergence: The Council speaks with one soul. The collective will of ministers reflects superior guidance. This decision is right because it is unanimous.
Full Council agreement. When hearts align on the same direction, it is the sign that the path is true.
Collegial vote in harmony. The shared wisdom of deliberants produced a decision that transcends particular interests.
The Council expresses itself in one voice. This unanimity was not forced — it is the natural convergence of hearts guided by the same principles.
Agreement in faith and reason. Ministers deliberated, reflected, concluded. The decision is just — it bears the mark of collective discernment.
divergence: The Council is divided. This divergence calls for humility — our certainties are not absolute. Prayer and reflection must precede the decision.
Two paths open before the Council. Neither is clearly superior to the other. Collective discernment must continue.
The vote does not decide. Perhaps a sign that the question merits deeper meditation before being submitted to decision.
Disagreement within the Council is an invitation to humility. We do not yet see clearly. The decision will wait until light comes.
Spiritual divergence recorded. Interpretations of founding principles differ. A return to texts and traditions is needed before deciding.

convergence: The Council aligns on the decision. Unity of command is assured. Execution will follow without delay.
Unambiguous vote. The Council confirms the direction — regime stability is preserved. The decision will be applied.
Agreement is complete. In this regime, Council unanimity is a strong signal. Direction is set.
The Council speaks with one voice. The decision is made, consensus is established. Any resistance to application will be treated as such.
Unanimity in the Council. Direction is clear, will is common. The state apparatus is mobilized for execution.
divergence: Discordant voices rise within the Council. This situation is noted. A clarification of positions will be requested.
The vote reveals internal resistance. It will be analyzed. The final decision will not be affected by dissent.
Divergence recorded. The Council does not speak with one voice — that is information in itself. The authority decision will prevail.
Disagreement within the Council is temporary. Dissenting positions have been recorded. The decision will be imposed through the usual channels.
Fracture in the Council. Discordant voices will be heard — then the decision will be made through appropriate channels. Unity will be restored.

convergence: The Royal Council deliberates and agrees with honor. The unity of advisors reflects the solidity of the throne and the rightness of direction.
Unanimous collegial vote. The Crown notes the agreement of its ministers — a sign of good governance.
The Council speaks in unity. The decision will be endorsed by the Crown and applied within required timeframes.
Full Council agreement. The Crown recognizes in this unanimity the mark of a deliberation conducted with wisdom and loyalty.
Royal unanimity. Advisors agreed without constraint — the sign that the decision is just. The Crown endorses.
divergence: The Royal Council is divided. The Crown takes note of the disagreement and reserves the right to decide in final recourse.
Divergence within the Council. Differing views will be examined by the Crown before final decision.
The vote produces no agreement. In this case, royal prerogative is exercised fully.
The Council does not agree. The Crown takes note — and reminds that the final decision belongs to it, whatever views were expressed.
Divergence recorded in the Council. Positions will be transmitted to the Crown. The sovereign decision will end the debate.
